emails.subject_line: something we noticed about pratt regional's revenue picture
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

Read your comments about PRMC's stabilization plan. Pulling through a default notice and demonstrating a profitable quarter takes real discipline.

For a sole community provider at PRMC's scale, $11M+ in AR over 90 days is a structural problem - not just a collections issue. Coding gaps in Medicare and Medicaid claims compound that over time.

We work with critical access and sole community providers to close those gaps without adding headcount.

Open to a 15-minute call this week?

emails.subject_line: a thought about pratt regional's ar recovery
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

Following up from last week. PRMC has operated at a loss for nearly a decade - but the county's decision to keep the hospital running shows community commitment.

A comparable sole community provider in the Midwest had $9M in aged AR and declining Medicare reimbursement. We helped them recover $1.4M in year one by targeting their top denial categories and improving coding accuracy on complex cases.

Can I grab 10 minutes to show you what we found in their claims data - and how it maps to PRMC's situation?

emails.subject_line: pratt regional's stabilization - one more angle
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

Last note. PRMC's long-term stabilization depends on squeezing every dollar out of legitimate reimbursement. With Medicare and Medicaid rates under pressure and $11M in aged receivables, coding accuracy is a lever you control.

We've helped three similar-sized community hospitals improve their first-pass rate by 30%+ in under six months.

Last shot: Tuesday or Thursday work for a quick 15-minute demo of what we're seeing in comparable claims data?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about pratt regional's revenue picture
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

Read your comments about PRMC's stabilization plan. Getting through a default notice with a profitable quarter to show is no small thing.

For a sole community provider at PRMC's scale, $11M+ in AR over 90 days often signals a coding accuracy problem upstream - not just a collections issue. That's recoverable.

Happy to share how a comparable sole community provider in the Midwest handled this. Worth 15 minutes?

emails.subject_line: a thought about pratt regional's ar recovery
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

Following up. PRMC has weathered a decade of operating losses - and the county's recommitment gives you a real window to stabilize.

A CFO at a 40-bed community hospital similar to PRMC cut their denial rate by 38% and recovered $1.4M in year one by fixing coding accuracy on their top Medicare service lines.

A CFO at a similar-sized sole community provider solved this differently. Want me to walk you through it?

emails.subject_line: pratt regional's stabilization - one more angle
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

One more note. PRMC's path to sustainability runs through reimbursement optimization - there's real money in the claims data that doesn't require new patients or new programs.

Three community hospitals I've worked with approached their AR and coding problems differently. One approach recovered 2x what the others did in the same period.

Three community hospital CFOs solved this differently. Quick call to compare approaches?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about insight health systems' operations
emails.email_body:
Hi Ali,

Saw you're speaking at Becker's on ASC business and operations. That context made something click.

A COO at a multi-site ASC group in the Midwest was losing 12% of net revenue to coding gaps across surgical specialties. No one had flagged it - the billers were busy, the volumes were up, but the margin wasn't moving.

When they ran a targeted audit of their top five procedure codes, they found $2.1M in recoverable revenue in 90 days. The fix didn't require new staff.

Curious whether Insight Health Systems is prioritizing coding accuracy or denial management as the bigger operational lever right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight health's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hey Ali,

Following up from last week. Different angle on the same challenge.

A VP of Operations at a Chicago-area ambulatory network - similar complexity to Insight Health Systems - had a denial rate sitting at 11% for 18 months. Their team had tried three different clearinghouses. The problem wasn't the clearinghouse.

It was upstream: physician documentation in outpatient surgical cases wasn't mapping cleanly to the procedure codes being billed. One workflow change cut denials by 60% in a single quarter.

Is denial rate reduction something your team is actively working through right now?

emails.subject_line: before becker's - a quick note for ali
emails.email_body:
Hello Ali,

Last note before Becker's. Bridging back to where these stories land for Insight Health Systems specifically.

Both situations I described - the ASC group and the ambulatory network - had the same root cause: a gap between what physicians documented and what got billed. At Insight's scale across your sites, that gap compounds.

We've helped organizations like yours close it within two quarters, without replacing any existing systems.

Would it help to see how similar multi-site organizations approached this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about insight health systems' operations
emails.email_body:
Hi Ali,

Saw you're speaking at Becker's on ASC business and operations - which made a counterintuitive question come to mind.

Why do high-volume surgical practices often have lower coding accuracy than lower-volume ones?

It's not what you'd expect. And the answer has a direct dollar figure attached to it for multi-site operations like Insight Health Systems.

Curious whether Insight is prioritizing throughput optimization or revenue per case right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight health's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hey Ali,

Following up on that question. Here's part of the answer.

High-volume surgical environments create documentation speed pressure. Physicians prioritize throughput, and the coding specificity that drives reimbursement accuracy gets compressed.

One multi-site ASC group we worked with discovered that 14% of their surgical cases had documentation that didn't support the codes being billed - invisible until an audit surfaced $2.4M in recoverable claims.

Is undercoded surgical revenue something your team is tracking at Insight?

emails.subject_line: the full picture for insight health systems
emails.email_body:
Hello Ali,

Here's the complete answer to the question I raised two weeks ago.

High-volume practices have lower coding accuracy because speed and specificity trade off - and no one's watching that tradeoff in real time. The organizations that fix it don't slow down throughput. They add a layer of automated review that catches the gaps before claims go out.

For Insight Health Systems' scale, that's typically $1-3M in year-one recovery.

Would it help to see how similar multi-site ASC organizations approached this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about johns hopkins hospital's operations
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare operations. Your work as Executive-in-Residence at Pitt adds a lens most COOs don't have - translating academic insight into operational practice.

At a system Johns Hopkins' complexity, coding accuracy in high-acuity cases often lags documentation quality. That gap quietly costs top academic medical centers 6-10% of net revenue annually.

We work with complex academic systems to close that gap without disrupting existing workflows.

Curious whether Johns Hopkins is prioritizing coding accuracy or denial prevention as the higher-leverage initiative right now?

P.S. - We offer a free 30-day pilot with your actual claims. We are HITRUST, SOC II, and HIPAA Compliant.

emails.subject_line: a thought about johns hopkins' revenue cycle complexity
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Following up from last week. Your ACHE service award recognizes sustained leadership - and sustainable systems require sustainable revenue.

One academic medical center we worked with - comparable complexity to Johns Hopkins - had a 9% denial rate on their highest-reimbursement DRGs. Running AI-assisted coding review on those specific cases cut denials by 44% in two quarters and recovered $5.7M.

The CMO described it as finally having visibility into the revenue side of clinical documentation.

Is denial management on complex inpatient cases something your operations team is actively focused on?

P.S. - We are HITRUST, SOC II, and HIPAA Compliant.

emails.subject_line: april - one more thought before we move on
emails.email_body:
Hello April,

Last note. For a system Johns Hopkins' scale and reputation, revenue leakage from coding gaps isn't just a financial issue - it's a data integrity issue that affects benchmarking, quality scores, and payer negotiations.

Three academic medical centers I've worked with each approached this differently. The approach that combined clinical documentation improvement with automated coding review outperformed the others by 2.5x in year-one recovery.

Would it help to see how similar academic systems approached this?

Read your post about the talent in healthcare - your point about being constantly reminded of it resonates, especially given what you're overseeing with WVU Medicine's expansion.

Absorbing Independence Health System's five hospitals while migrating to Epic is the kind of integration where revenue cycle complexity compounds fast. Coding consistency across legacy and new systems is typically where margin leaks first.

We work with health systems navigating multi-hospital integrations to protect revenue through the transition.

Curious whether WVU Medicine is prioritizing coding consistency or denial prevention as the higher-risk area during the Independence integration?

Hey Carrie,

Following up from last week. The $800M commitment to modernize Independence Health is significant - and the Epic implementation adds execution risk on the revenue side.

One health system we worked with during a comparable multi-hospital Epic migration saw denial rates spike 18% in the first 90 days post-go-live. They had a plan for the clinical side; the revenue cycle caught them by surprise.

We helped them recover $3.8M that year by targeting the highest-denial service lines first.

Is revenue cycle continuity during the Independence integration something your team is actively planning for?

Read your post thanking Baker Tilly as HFMA's Gold Sponsor. Finance leadership in a $4.9B academic system with $110M in operating losses requires navigating serious complexity.

Professional revenue cycle at academic medical centers typically carries 8-14% in coding-related denial exposure - and OHSU's pressure on expenses outpacing revenue makes every recoverable dollar count.

We focus specifically on professional billing accuracy for academic health systems.

Curious whether OHSU is prioritizing professional coding accuracy or physician documentation improvement as the higher-leverage area right now?

emails.email_body:
Hey Tammy,

Following up. OHSU's $110M operating loss in FY2024 and S&P downgrade reflect a system where expenses are structurally outpacing revenue. Professional billing accuracy is a direct lever.

A comparable academic medical center's professional revenue cycle team reduced their denial rate from 13% to under 5% in two quarters using AI-assisted coding review on their top 20 physician specialties. They recovered $3.4M that year.

emails.email_body:

Last outreach. OHSU is entering FY2026 under financial pressure with a clear mandate to stabilize margins. Professional revenue cycle is one of the faster levers to pull.

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about ohsu's professional revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hi Tammy,

Read your post thanking Baker Tilly as HFMA's Gold Sponsor. Quick question that might seem odd given your role at OHSU.

Why do academic medical centers with the best-trained physicians often have the worst professional billing accuracy?

It's counterintuitive - and the answer has a direct dollar figure attached to it for systems like OHSU.

Curious whether OHSU is prioritizing professional coding accuracy or physician documentation as the more urgent problem right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about ohsu's professional billing performance
emails.email_body:
Hey Tammy,

Following up on that question. Here's part of the answer.

Academic physicians document for clinical and research purposes - not billing specificity. The more complex the case, the wider the gap between what was done and what gets coded. OHSU's case complexity is high and rising post-pandemic.

One academic medical center running a comparable professional billing operation found $3.4M in recoverable claims in their top 20 specialties - money that had already been denied or undercoded.

Is professional coding accuracy something your team is tracking systematically at OHSU?

emails.subject_line: the full picture for ohsu's professional revenue
emails.email_body:
Hello Tammy,

Here's the complete answer. Academic physicians document for complexity, not reimbursement. That gap between clinical documentation and billing codes costs OHSU-level systems $3-8M annually in professional revenue - systematically, quietly, and mostly in your highest-acuity cases.

The fix doesn't require changing physician behavior. It requires a layer of automated review before claims go out.

Would it help to see how similar academic medical centers approached this problem?

Read your LinkedIn post - your "Amen!" reaction speaks to something clinical leaders across community hospitals feel right now. The gap between what gets done at the bedside and what gets captured in the revenue cycle is real.

Morris Hospital's decision to exit trauma Level II designation while managing a recent data breach recovery means operational focus is stretched. Revenue cycle is often where the gaps appear first.

emails.email_body:
Hi John,

Read your post about MetroHealth's Trauma System 101 Community Town Hall. The outreach work at MetroHealth reflects a system investing in community trust alongside clinical excellence.

MetroHealth is projecting a $31.2M budget shortfall in 2026, down from $48.3M in 2025. That improvement is real progress, but $446M in charity care puts sustained pressure on revenue cycle margins.

We work with health systems facing that exact tension between mission and margin.

Would it make sense to explore how others have navigated this?

emails.subject_line: a thought on metrohealth's margin path
emails.email_body:
Hey John,

MetroHealth's charity care costs have doubled since 2022, now exceeding $1M per day. With county subsidies declining and Medicaid uncertainty growing, the margin on insured patient revenue has to carry more weight.

We've seen health systems in comparable positions recover 15-20% more on professional claims by addressing coding throughput, without adding billing staff.

If this resonates, happy to share more on what that looked like for a similar-size system.

emails.subject_line: metrohealth's revenue cycle and what's next
emails.email_body:
Hello John,

MetroHealth is navigating $50M in 2024 operating losses while investing in Magnet-level nursing, ED renovations, and new outpatient expansion. That's a system under financial strain while simultaneously growing.

Systems in that position need revenue cycle efficiency to scale without proportional cost increases. We've mapped exactly this dynamic for organizations like MetroHealth.

No worries either way, but figured it was worth flagging given what's on your plate in 2026.

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at metrohealth
emails.email_body:
Hi John,

Read your post about MetroHealth's Trauma System 101 Community Town Hall. The community trust MetroHealth is building sits alongside a $31.2M projected shortfall in 2026.

Most health systems in this position underestimate how much revenue cycle inefficiency is amplifying the gap. $446M in charity care compresses margin on every covered claim you do collect.

We've built a framework for health systems navigating this specific tension.

Worth sending over our revenue cycle efficiency benchmark report for safety-net systems?

emails.subject_line: a framework for metrohealth's margin challenge
emails.email_body:
Hey John,

MetroHealth's charity care costs doubled since 2022. With county subsidies declining from $35M to $32M in 2026, insured revenue cycle performance carries the entire margin story.

We put together a revenue recovery playbook specifically for public/safety-net health systems managing $400M+ in uncompensated care. It covers coding automation, denial patterns, and AR velocity benchmarks.

We put together this playbook for health systems like MetroHealth. Want a copy?

emails.subject_line: what a comparable system did with this
emails.email_body:
Hello John,

A safety-net health system with a comparable charity care burden used our AR recovery breakdown to identify $8M in recoverable revenue they had essentially written off. No new staff, no new contracts.

Given MetroHealth's 2026 budget constraints and the $31.2M shortfall, that kind of recovery changes the margin math meaningfully.

Happy to share the breakdown we used with that peer organization.

emails.subject_line: april's coding throughput at johns hopkins
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare operations. Your work bridging quality and operational excellence at Hopkins is clear.

Hospitals at Hopkins' scale typically have coding throughput gaps that sit invisible inside otherwise strong operational metrics. The larger the system, the harder it is to see where revenue cycle velocity is slowing.

We work with COOs at academic medical centers to surface exactly these gaps.

Curious whether Hopkins is prioritizing coding accuracy or throughput speed right now?

emails.subject_line: april's revenue cycle velocity at jhmi
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Your ACHE service recognition and EIR role at Pitt Public Health signal a leader focused on system-level change, not just operational maintenance.

At AMCs like Hopkins, coding backlogs quietly compound. A 5% denial rate on inpatient volume at your scale represents tens of millions annually. Most COOs don't see it until quarter-end.

We've helped similar organizations reduce denials by 20%+ and cut coding lag by 30%.

Is coding capacity something your revenue cycle team is actively measuring?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins' revenue cycle gap
emails.email_body:
Hello April,

Academic medical centers consistently leave revenue on the table in professional coding due to volume and complexity. Hopkins runs one of the highest-acuity patient mixes in the country.

We have a breakdown of how comparable AMCs closed their coding gaps without adding FTEs. Given your operational focus and what you've built at Hopkins, I think it maps directly to your situation.

Would it help to see how similar organizations approached this?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + coding capacity
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare leadership. Your operational lens at Hopkins is evident.

Academic medical centers at Hopkins' scale face a structural coding capacity problem: patient complexity outpaces coder bandwidth, and denials compound silently. High-acuity case mix means every missed code has outsized revenue impact.

We work with COOs at similar AMCs to close that gap without adding headcount.

Curious whether Hopkins is prioritizing coding accuracy or throughput capacity right now?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + staffing gaps in revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Your ACHE award and EIR role signal a systems thinker. Most COOs at AMCs don't discover their coding staffing gap until it shows up as a denial trend, by then it's 60-90 days behind.

We've documented that AMCs similar to Hopkins recover 15-20% more on complex inpatient claims when coding throughput is automated at the back end.

Is revenue cycle staffing coverage something your team is actively tracking right now?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + claim lag
emails.email_body:
Hello April,

High-complexity AMCs routinely lose 3-5% of net revenue to claim lag and coding backlog, not denial management. It's the quieter leak.

We've worked with COOs at comparable systems to cut coding lag by 30% and improve clean claim rates measurably. Given what you've built operationally at Hopkins, this maps directly to your priorities.

Would it help to see how similar organizations closed this specific gap?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at pratt regional
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

PRMC just turned a profitable quarter after nearly a decade of losses, and the county pulled back its lease termination notice. That reversal signals real momentum.

Most rural critical access hospitals in that position still carry $11M+ in aging AR. If that backlog persists, it offsets every operational gain you've made.

We work with CFOs at similarly-sized sole community providers to recover revenue from accounts 90+ days past due without adding headcount.

Curious whether PRMC is prioritizing AR recovery or cost containment right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought on your path forward, alan
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

Your press quote on PRMC's stabilization plan stood out. As CFO navigating a decade of losses while managing Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement compression, the margin for error is near zero.

Leaders in your seat typically face a compounding problem: the staff capacity to chase denials and aging AR shrinks right when the volume matters most.

We've helped CFOs recover 15-20% of write-off-ready AR with no upfront cost.

Is reducing that $11M AR backlog something you're actively working on this quarter?

emails.subject_line: what other rural hospital cfos are doing
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

Other CFOs at sole community providers in the Midwest facing similar reimbursement pressure have shifted AR recovery from a staffing problem to a technology problem. The ones seeing results are capturing 20-30% more on accounts they had essentially written off.

With PRMC's hiring freeze still in place, adding coding or billing staff isn't viable. But autonomous AR recovery doesn't require headcount.

Would it help to see how a comparable rural hospital approached this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at pratt regional
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

PRMC has $11M+ in AR more than 90 days past due over the last two years. For a 35-bed hospital running on thin margins after a decade of losses, that backlog is material.

Decreased Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement rates plus low utilization makes revenue cycle efficiency the one lever that doesn't require volume growth.

We work with CFOs at similar-sized facilities on exactly this challenge.

Curious whether PRMC is prioritizing AR recovery or reducing denials at the front end?

emails.subject_line: digging deeper into pratt regional's ar challenge
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

The $11M AR backlog at PRMC compounds in a specific way: accounts past 90 days have recovery rates that drop 30-40% each additional month. At current Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement rates, write-offs accelerate faster than new revenue can offset them.

Facilities your size with a hiring freeze can't add billing staff. The math only works if recovery becomes more efficient per claim, not per person.

Is this kind of AR aging curve something your team is actively modeling?

emails.subject_line: a specific recommendation for prmc
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

For a sole community provider at PRMC's scale, the highest-ROI move is autonomous coding and claim re-processing on the 90+ day bucket. Facilities similar to yours have recovered 15-25% of accounts that were queued for write-off, without adding staff.

Given PRMC's profitable last quarter and the county's renewed commitment, this is the window to convert AR momentum into sustained margin.

Would it help to see how similar organizations approached this specifically?
